speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Based on your recent stress test and coronary angiogram, I’m diagnosing you with Coronary Artery Disease, confirmed as of August 19th.

speaker: doctor
text: To help manage your blood pressure and reduce cardiac workload, I’m starting you on Amlodipine 10mg once daily, effective today.

speaker: patient
text: Thank you, Doctor. I’ve been trying to monitor how I feel—mostly fatigue and some shortness of breath when walking up stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important context. Have you noticed any swelling—especially in your ankles or legs?

speaker: patient
text: Yes—I’ve had noticeable swelling in both lower legs for about three days now.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s check your vitals right away. I’ll also listen to your heart and lungs carefully.

speaker: patient
text: The lower limb edema has gotten a bit worse since yesterday—I had to loosen my shoes by midday.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is elevated at 119 beats per minute—this is concerning given your CAD diagnosis and current symptoms.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose is 5.6 mmol/L—well within the normal fasting range.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is quite low at 63 mmHg—this, combined with the tachycardia and edema, suggests possible volume overload or medication-related effects.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been feeling lightheaded when standing quickly—could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: Given the edema, tachycardia, and borderline hypotension, I’m going to switch you from Amlodipine to Lisinopril 20mg once daily—it’s better suited for your profile and may improve fluid balance.

speaker: patient
text: Okay—I’ll start that today. Should I stop the Amlodipine completely?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, discontinue Amlodipine as of tonight. I’ll also order an echocardiogram and BNP to assess for heart failure exacerbation.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been checking my pulse at home—sometimes it feels really fast, especially in the afternoon.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll monitor that closely. Let’s recheck your heart rate before you leave—and we’ll schedule a follow-up in five days.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also been tracking my glucose at home—it’s been stable, no highs or lows.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s reassuring. Consistent glucose control supports overall cardiovascular health.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is now 104 bpm—still elevated but down from earlier this morning.

speaker: patient
text: That’s the same number I saw this morning—the 5.6 mmol/L reading matches what you recorded earlier.
